era_context: Continuation of Neo-Assyrian expansion under Shalmaneser III. The Levantine kingdoms remain under Assyrian pressure. Egypt continues its fragmentation. Greek Geometric period continues; early Phoenician expansion in the Mediterranean. The Zhou Dynasty in China is in the Spring and Autumn period (traditional dating places this later, but chronological uncertainty is significant for this era).
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (Chinese chronology for this period is disputed)

events.title: Continuation of Shalmaneser III's military campaigns
events.description: Shalmaneser III continued his systematic annual campaigns into the Levant, maintaining Assyrian hegemony and extracting tribute from vassal states. These campaigns were integral to Assyrian imperial strategy and are documented in royal inscriptions. The campaigns reinforced Assyrian control over trade routes and prevented coalition-building among Levantine kingdoms.
events.certainty_note: Shalmaneser III's reign is well-documented, and annual campaigns are attested. The specific year 808 BCE falls within his documented campaign sequence, though individual years are not always distinguished in surviving sources.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are definitively attested for 808 BCE. The year is assigned to Shalmaneser III's reign by chronological convention, but no contemporary source explicitly marks 808 BCE as distinct from surrounding years. The significance of this year is historiographic rather than evidentially grounded.
historiographic_note: 808 BCE is poorly documented. No specific events are known for this year with certainty. The historical record for this period is dominated by Assyrian royal inscriptions, which provide a court-centered, military-focused narrative. Civilian, economic, and cultural developments are largely invisible. The year is included in this chronicle primarily to maintain chronological continuity rather than because of documented historical significance.

graph_edges.note: Part of the same campaign sequence as 809 BCE
